fix: Same as BSP-1's structural half — fail closed + startup completeness assertion. Tracked separately because it warrants a guardrail/test even after BSP-1's data is fixed.

title: Admin host does not register CustomerOnly policy; shared PolicyBuilder maps several permissions to it
where: src/Cleansia.Web.Admin/Extensions/ServiceExtensions.cs:200-231 (registers only Authenticated, EmployeeOrAdmin, AdminOnly, OwnerOrElevated — not CustomerOnly); PolicyBuilder.Map maps CanCancelOrder, CanSubmitOrderReview, CanManageMembership to CustomerOnly
impact: If any admin controller ever references a CustomerOnly-mapped permission, the authorization middleware throws "No policy found" (500), not a clean 403. Latent today (admin controllers don't use those permissions) but a sharp edge from sharing one global map across hosts with divergent registered policy sets.
fix: Register the full PhysicalPolicy set on every host (or host-scope the map). Add a startup check that every value in PolicyBuilder.Map is a policy registered on that host.

title: JWT signing key is shared across all audiences; only the aud claim separates trust domains
where: each host's AddJwt (ValidAudience = JwtAudiences.Admin/Partner/Customer/Mobile) with common JwtSettings:Secret and ValidIssuer = "cleansia"; RefreshToken.cs:61-66
impact: Audience isolation holds today (enforced at validation and issuance). The structural risk: a single leaked JwtSettings:Secret forges tokens for every audience including Admin. With one shared HMAC key, audience is the only wall.
fix: Separate signing key (or asymmetric keys) for the Admin audience; document the audience-isolation invariant. Key rotation is owner-only — escalate key changes to owner.

title: Anonymous Order/LookupBatch returns tenant-scoped order data with a batch shape
where: src/Cleansia.Web.Customer/Controllers/OrderController.cs:29-37 ([AllowAnonymous] LookupOrderBatch); LookupOrderBatch.Query
impact: Single Lookup requires orderNumber+email (a shared-secret pair, OK). The batch variant must enforce the same per-item secret and a length cap; otherwise it is an enumeration surface that bypasses the global tenant filter (anonymous = no tenant claim, per S3). Flagged for the order-subsystem audit to verify LookupOrderBatch.Query validates each (orderNumber,email) pair and caps batch size.
fix: Verify Order/LookupBatch enforces per-item secret + batch cap; route to order-subsystem audit.

title: Google sign-in trusts client-supplied email/GoogleId instead of verified token claims (account takeover)
where: src/Cleansia.Core.AppServices/Features/Auth/GoogleAuth.cs:59-75 (validator) and :93-103 (handler); also GoogleAuth.cs:63-66 (IsDevelopment bypass); hosts src/Cleansia.Web.Customer/Controllers/AuthController.cs:51 and src/Cleansia.Web.Mobile.Partner/Controllers/AuthController.cs:59
impact: ValidateGoogleUserAsync calls GoogleJsonWebSignature.ValidateAsync but throws away the result, never comparing the verified token's Email/Subject against command.Email/command.GoogleId. The handler looks the user up by the body-supplied command.Email and issues a full JWT. An attacker presents a genuine Google token for their own throwaway account but sets Email: victim@x.com in the body and receives a valid session for the victim (provided victim's AuthenticationType == Google). Direct, no-credential account takeover of every Google-auth user on both customer web and partner mobile hosts. Secondary: 'if (_googleConfig.IsDevelopment) return true;' bypasses all signature validation when the dev flag is set, making auth fully forgeable on a config slip.
fix: Validate the token first, then bind identity to the payload: var payload = await GoogleJsonWebSignature.ValidateAsync(token, settings) with Audience restricted to your Google client id; require payload.EmailVerified; resolve the user by payload.Subject (Google sub)/payload.Email, never from the request body. Drop Email/GoogleId from the command or treat them as untrusted and overwrite. Remove the IsDevelopment short-circuit or gate it behind a test-only fake validator registered in DI. Fix touches the DTO contract -> manual_step: nswag-regen.

title: Auth rate limiter is a single global bucket, not per-IP/per-account (brute-force unthrottled; global DoS)
where: src/Cleansia.Config/Abstractions/CleansiaStartupBase.cs:76-92
impact: AddFixedWindowLimiter("auth", ...) { PermitLimit = 10 } is configured with no partition key, so it is one bucket shared by all callers. The comment '10 req/min/partition' is false: no per-IP or per-account isolation, so distributed credential-stuffing isn't throttled relative to the global pool and a 6-digit confirmation/reset code can be ground down. Worse, any single client can spend the 10/min on /Login and lock out every legitimate login/register/reset across the whole API for the rest of each window (trivial global DoS). Affects every endpoint tagged [EnableRateLimiting("auth")] across all five hosts.
fix: Replace named limiters with options.AddPolicy("auth", httpContext => RateLimitPartition.GetFixedWindowLimiter(partitionKey: <client IP, or IP+email for login>, ...)) so each partition gets its own 10/min window. Partition on HttpContext.Connection.RemoteIpAddress (honoring X-Forwarded-For behind the proxy) for anonymous auth routes and on the user id for authenticated mutations. Keep QueueLimit = 0. Add an integration test asserting client A's 429s don't affect client B.

title: Email-confirmation and password-reset codes are 6-digit non-cryptographic numbers, looked up by code alone (brute-forceable; reset -> takeover)
where: src/Cleansia.Core.Domain/Users/User.cs:95-96, 113-116, 173-176 (code generation); src/Cleansia.Infra.Database/Repositories/UserRepository.cs:45-48 (GetByConfirmationCodeAsync code-only lookup); ConfirmUserEmail.cs:35-53; ChangePassword.cs reset path
impact: Both ConfirmationCode and ResetPasswordCode are Random.Shared.Next(100000, 999999) — a 900k-space, non-cryptographic PRNG (predictable from observed outputs), valid 15 minutes. ConfirmUserEmail resolves the user by the code alone (GetByConfirmationCodeAsync(code)) with no email/identifier binding, so an attacker needn't target a specific account; any outstanding code confirms and logs in (ConfirmUserEmail returns a JWT) whichever account holds it. Combined with the broken rate limiter (IDA-SEC-02), 900k guesses are feasible. The reset code binds to email but is the same weak 6-digit space and directly resets the password -> takeover.
fix: Generate codes with a CSPRNG. For password reset and email confirmation use a high-entropy URL-safe token (>=128 bits, RandomNumberGenerator), store only a hash, and look up reset by (email, token). If a 6-digit OTP UX is required, bind it to the account, cap attempts per code (e.g. 5) and invalidate on exhaustion, and fix the rate limiter. Never resolve a session-issuing flow by code alone. Touches schema (token columns/hashes) -> manual_step: ef-migration.

title: Any Employee can read any user's full PII by id (no ownership check; OwnerOrElevated grants all employees)
where: src/Cleansia.Web.Partner/Controllers/UserController.cs:28-39 (GetById -> GetUser.Query), handler src/Cleansia.Core.AppServices/Features/Users/GetUser.cs:31-41, policy map src/Cleansia.Core.AppServices/Authentication/PolicyBuilder.cs:48 (CanViewUserDetail = OwnerOrElevated), policy impl src/Cleansia.Web.Partner/Extensions/ServiceExtensions.cs:211-228
impact: GetUser.Handler returns a UserItem containing Email, PhoneNumber, FirstName, LastName, Id for an arbitrary UserId with no ownership or relationship check. The gating policy OwnerOrElevated returns true for any Employee (user.IsInRole(Employee) -> true), not just admins. Any cleaner can enumerate every customer's email and phone number in their tenant by iterating ids — a wholesale PII harvest. Tenant boundary holds (ExistsAsync filter, in-tenant only), but that's still every customer. The Policy.cs:50 comment 'Authenticated (All roles)' also mismatches the actual OwnerOrElevated mapping.
fix: Map this lookup-any-user-by-id (an admin/support capability) to AdminOnly, or add an explicit relationship check in the handler (e.g. the employee is assigned to an order for that customer) before returning contact PII. If employees legitimately need a customer's contact info for an assigned job, expose only that narrow, order-scoped field set, not the full UserItem.

title: PII and the confirmation code are written to logs at Warning level
where: src/Cleansia.Core.AppServices/Features/Auth/ConfirmUserEmail.cs:41 and :47-48; also Register.cs:102-111 (ReferralCode)
impact: _logger.LogWarning("Email confirmation failed: no user found with code {Code}", command.Code) logs the confirmation code (a session-issuing secret) at Warning, and the expiry branch logs user.Email plus the code at Warning. Codes and email land in centralized log storage (queryable, longer-retained, broader access than the DB) — a logged code is replayable within its 15-min TTL by anyone with log access, and the email is plain PII. Register.cs:102-111 also logs ReferralCode at Information/Warning (lower sensitivity, but still a user-entered token).
fix: Never log confirmation/reset codes at any level. Log the failure with a non-reversible discriminator (a hash prefix or just 'code not found'/'code expired') and userId instead of user.Email. Drop the {Code} and {Email} template args; demote any unavoidable PII to LogDebug.

title: Customer/Partner refresh-token rotation does not re-check the user's profile, only the audience
where: src/Cleansia.Web.Customer/Controllers/AuthController.cs:90, src/Cleansia.Web.Partner/Controllers/AuthController.cs:101, src/Cleansia.Web.Mobile.Partner/Controllers/AuthController.cs:106, handler src/Cleansia.Core.AppServices/Features/Auth/RefreshToken.cs:75-79
impact: RefreshToken.Handler only enforces RequiredProfile when the controller sets it. The Admin host sets RequiredProfile = Administrator (AdminAuthController.cs:48), but Customer, Partner, and both mobile hosts set only RequiredAudience. Login (customer) enforces no profile at login, so an Administrator/Employee authenticating through the customer host gets a customer-audience refresh token, and nothing on the refresh path re-validates that the bearer's role still matches the host's intended audience population. A partner-audience refresh will re-mint for a user whose profile was demoted to Customer because only audience is checked. The existing re-load + IsActive check (:68-73) is good and should be the template.
fix: Have each host pass the RequiredProfile it expects (Customer host: Customer; Partner host: Employee/Administrator) so refresh re-pins role on every rotation, mirroring the Admin host. Equivalently, validate the role claim against the host's allowed set in the controller before Mediator.Send.

title: JwtTokenResponse.HasAdminAccess defaults to true; only AdminLogin sets it explicitly
where: src/Cleansia.Core.AppServices/Shared/DTOs/ResponseModels/JwtTokenResponse.cs:16 (bool HasAdminAccess = true); set only in AdminLogin.cs:101; left default in Login, PartnerLogin, GoogleAuth, ConfirmUserEmail, RefreshToken (RefreshToken.cs:91)
impact: The DTO's HasAdminAccess positional default is true, so every token-issuing path that doesn't override it (customer login, partner login, Google, email-confirm, and crucially RefreshToken for the admin host) returns HasAdminAccess = true. API authorization does enforce roles server-side, but the admin SPA uses this exact flag to decide whether to show the admin shell, so a non-admin who reaches an admin-host token (or an admin user whose token is refreshed) gets an inconsistent/over-permissive client signal. A 'UI hint' that fails open to true is the wrong default for an admin-access flag.
fix: Default HasAdminAccess = false and have every issuer compute it from the user's profile (or remove it entirely and let the client read the Role field, which is already returned). Ensure the admin RefreshToken path sets it from the reloaded user. DTO default change -> manual_step: nswag-regen.

title: Admin GDPR delete/export and admin-user deactivate have no self/last-admin protection
where: src/Cleansia.Core.AppServices/Features/Gdpr/AdminDeleteUserAccount.cs:15-24 (validator only checks existence), src/Cleansia.Web.Admin/Controllers/AdminUserController.cs:79-92 (DeactivateAdminUser), AdminDeleteUserAccount.Handler
impact: AdminDeleteUserAccount accepts any in-tenant UserId and anonymizes/deactivates it with no guard against the actor deleting their own admin account or the last remaining administrator. An admin can GDPR-delete (irreversibly anonymize via User.Anonymize()) another administrator, or deactivate the final admin, locking the tenant out of its own admin console with no recovery path. There is no 'can't target an Administrator via the GDPR customer-deletion tool' check, even though that tool is meant for customer/employee data-subject requests.
fix: In the admin-delete/deactivate validators, reject when target.Profile == Administrator (admins are managed only through the AdminUsers feature), reject self-deletion, and reject deactivating/deleting the last active administrator in the tenant. Add the same last-admin guard to DeactivateAdminUser. Warrants a small user story 'Protect admin accounts from self/last-admin deletion & deactivation.'

title: OwnerOrElevated owner-branch is dead code for the only endpoint that uses it (route key mismatch)
where: src/Cleansia.Web.Partner/Extensions/ServiceExtensions.cs:221-225 and the equivalent in each host; consumer UserController.cs:28-34
impact: The policy's customer/self branch reads http.Request.RouteValues["id"] and compares it to the caller's sub. But GetById binds GetUser.Query from query string (?UserId=), and the route has no {id} segment, so RouteValues["id"] is always null and the owner branch always returns false. Today this only removes access (customers can't reach it), so it's not an active hole, but the access model is silently not what the code intends; if anyone later adds a {id} route or relies on 'owner can read self,' the check will behave unexpectedly. It also masks IDA-SEC-04 (the elevated branch is what's actually granting everyone).
fix: Make the policy read the resource id from the same place the action binds it (or pass the id explicitly), and add a unit test that a customer can read only their own detail and an employee's access is governed by the intended rule (per IDA-SEC-04's resolution).

title: Anonymous confirm/reset/login flows are inert in multi-tenant mode (tenant filter excludes them)
where: tenant filter src/Cleansia.Infra.Database/CleansiaDbContext.cs:111-179; consumers GetByConfirmationCodeAsync/GetByEmailAsync via GetDbSet() in UserRepository.cs
impact: User : ITenantEntity, so all GetDbSet() reads get the global tenant filter. On [AllowAnonymous] routes there is no tenant_id claim, so GetCurrentTenantId() == null and the filter matches only rows where TenantId == null (single-tenant clause). In a real multi-tenant deployment (users with non-null TenantId), anonymous Login, ConfirmUserEmail, ResendConfirmationEmail, and password reset would find no user — the flows silently fail for all tenanted users. Security edge: a tempting 'fix' with IgnoreQueryFilters() would make confirm-by-code (IDA-SEC-03) match across all tenants, turning a weak code into a cross-tenant takeover primitive.
fix: Resolve the tenant on anonymous auth routes from a non-claim source (host/subdomain -> tenant) and set a tenant override before the lookup, rather than ignoring filters. If single-tenant is the only supported mode today, document that explicitly and add a guard test so a future multi-tenant rollout doesn't ship broken auth or an IgnoreQueryFilters cross-tenant leak. User story: 'Resolve tenant on anonymous auth endpoints without bypassing the global filter.'

title: GenerateInvoiceFunction is a no-op stub; generate-invoice queue is fully dead
where: src/Cleansia.Functions/Functions/GenerateInvoiceFunction.cs:11-27; QueueNames.cs:6; Messages/GenerateInvoiceMessage.cs
impact: The function deserializes the message and logs 'not yet implemented' then returns Task.CompletedTask. No producer enqueues generate-invoice (zero SendAsync calls). Invoice PDFs are instead generated synchronously inside the nightly timer (PayPeriodBackgroundService.GenerateInvoiceForEmployeeAsync), so a slow/failing PDF or email for one employee runs in the same loop as period closure, serializing all employees on one thread with no retry/DLQ. The stub is a maintenance trap: a future dev wiring a producer gets a silent no-op.
fix: Either (a) delete the function, GenerateInvoiceMessage, and QueueNames.GenerateInvoice and document that invoice gen is inline in the timer; or (b) finish the extraction — have PayPeriodBackgroundService enqueue one generate-invoice per employee and implement the consumer with the same idempotency guard as receipts. Decide one; don't ship the stub.

title: Enqueue-before-commit: CompleteOrder (and siblings) publish to 3 queues inside the handler, before the UnitOfWork pipeline commits
where: src/Cleansia.Core.AppServices/Features/Orders/CompleteOrder.cs:219, 227, 266; commit at UnitOfWorkPipelineBehavior.cs:19-20; also CreateOrder.cs:376, ConfirmRecurringOrder.cs:112, HandlePaymentNotification.cs:241
impact: queueClient.SendAsync for generate-receipt, notifications-dispatch, and calculate-order-pay all execute during the handler body; the DB CommitAsync runs after in the outer pipeline behavior. If the commit fails (or the process dies between enqueue and commit), consumers receive messages for an order whose Completed transition was never persisted: GenerateReceiptFunction/CalculateOrderPayFunction read the order in its pre-completion state, and the customer gets an 'All done!' push for an order that isn't done. There is no transactional outbox reconciling enqueue with the write. Violates runtime-readiness checklist item 4 and the S7/B8 reconciliation guarantee.
fix: Introduce a transactional outbox (write outbox rows in the same DbContext transaction; a dispatcher drains them post-commit), or at minimum move enqueues to a post-commit hook. Document the chosen ordering rule in consistency.md (extends B8).

title: No poison-queue / dead-letter consumer for ANY of the 5 queues; failures vanish silently after 5 dequeues
where: host.json (maxDequeueCount: 5, no per-queue override, no poison handler); no *-poison trigger anywhere in src
impact: GenerateReceipt, SendPushNotification, SendSitewidePromoFanout re-throw on failure to force queue retry (GenerateReceiptFunction.cs:107, SendPushNotificationFunction.cs:120, SendSitewidePromoFanoutFunction.cs:162). After 5 failures Azure auto-moves the message to <queue>-poison — but nothing consumes those poison queues and nothing alerts. A receipt that fails 5x (e.g. company-info misconfig, fiscal outage longer than retry window) is lost with no human-visible dead-end. Code comments repeatedly cite 'the poison-message pipeline' as if it handles this (SendSitewidePromoMessage.cs:23, CalculateOrderPayFunction.cs:59) — it does not exist. Violates runtime-readiness checklist item 6 and 'alert when a queue backs up.'
fix: Add a generic *-poison consumer that logs at Critical + raises an alert (App Insights) and optionally persists to a FailedMessage table for admin replay. Set per-queue maxDequeueCount where 5 is too low for outage windows (receipts).

title: GenerateReceiptFunction is not idempotent across an uncommitted retry: consumes a fiscal sequence number and uploads a blob before the single commit (S7)
where: GenerateReceiptFunction.cs:72-99; ReceiptService.GenerateReceiptAsync at ReceiptService.cs:49-95
impact: GenerateReceiptAsync calls GetNextSequenceForYearAsync (consumes a receipt number), receiptRepository.Add (in-memory only), and uploads the PDF to blob — all before the function's only CommitAsync (GenerateReceiptFunction.cs:99). The order.Receipt is not null guard (line 66) only protects across committed runs. If email-send (line 95) or the commit throws, the message retries and a brand-new receipt with a new sequence number is generated — producing a gap/duplicate in the legally-sequenced receipt numbering and potentially a second customer email. Directly violates S7.
fix: Make receipt creation idempotent on OrderId — reserve/lookup the receipt row in a committed step before email; only allocate a sequence number once per order; guard the email with receipt.EmailSent. Reuse the ReferralService/LoyaltyService ledger-check pattern named in S7.

title: Cron cadence mismatch: 4 notification/recurring timers run every 2 minutes, not the documented daily/30-min cadence
where: MaterializeRecurringBookingsFunction.cs:19; SendMembershipLifecycleNotificationsFunction.cs:24; SendRecurringOrderRemindersFunction.cs:23; SendNewJobsDigestTimerFunction.cs:24
impact: These are dev placeholders never promoted to production. Consequences: (1) the 'runs 30 min after Materialize' ordering guarantee is false — both fire on the same 2-min tick, so SendRecurringOrderReminders can sweep before that tick's materialization completes, missing same-day orders until the next tick; (2) 720 runs/day each = constant cross-tenant table scans (membership, orders, prefs) — wasted DB load and Function execution cost; (3) reliance on idempotency stamps to suppress duplicate user sends becomes load-bearing for correctness rather than a safety net. Idempotency stamps currently hold, so this is cost+correctness-of-ordering rather than user-visible spam today — but it's fragile.
fix: Promote to the documented production crons (use %AppSetting% cron syntax so dev/prod differ without code change). Re-establish the Materialize→Reminder ordering (Reminder at 02:30 after Materialize at 02:00).

title: FiscalRetryService commits the whole 50-receipt batch in one CommitAsync with no per-receipt durability; a commit failure loses all attempts and the timer never retries
where: FiscalRetryService.cs:38-93
impact: Per-receipt failures are swallowed (line 79-86, correct), but all state changes accumulate in one DbContext and commit once at line 91. If CommitAsync throws (DB blip), every receipt's retry-tracking/FiscalNextRetryAt/email-sent state in that batch is lost. Because this is a timer (not a queue), there is no automatic retry of the lost work — the next 5-min tick re-reads GetDueForRetryAsync and may re-send already-sent held emails (the email release at line 70-72 sets MarkEmailSent only in-memory until the failed commit). Partial S7 exposure on the held-email release.
fix: Commit per receipt (or in small chunks) inside the loop, or wrap the batch so a failed commit re-queues only the unprocessed remainder. Ensure the held-email release checks receipt.EmailSent post-commit.

title: SendPushNotification has no per-message idempotency; queue retry re-sends duplicate pushes
where: SendPushNotificationFunction.cs:91-120
impact: The function dispatches via FCM (line 91), then re-throws on any later failure (line 120) to force retry. FCM send is not deduplicated, so a failure after a successful FCM send (e.g. the dead-token prune commit at line 107 throws) causes the whole message to retry and the user gets the push twice. Pushes aren't financial, so this is Medium not High, but it's a real S7-class double-side-effect. Dead-token pruning only commits when InvalidTokens.Count > 0 (line 100-108) — correct.
fix: Either accept at-least-once for pushes explicitly (document it) and split the prune-commit so it can't trigger a re-send, or add a lightweight dedup key (event+user+window) the dispatcher checks.

title: SendSitewidePromoFanout re-throws and retries the ENTIRE campaign on any post-partial failure, with no resume cursor — duplicate fan-out
where: SendSitewidePromoFanoutFunction.cs:104-163
impact: Per-user enqueue failures are swallowed and skipped (line 137-146, good). But any failure outside that inner try (e.g. a ToListAsync page read throws on page 40 of 50, line 108) hits the outer catch and re-throws (line 162), so the queue retries the campaign from offset 0 — re-enqueuing pushes to all users already processed on the prior attempt. Up to 5x duplicate promo pushes to early-paged users on a flaky run. No persisted cursor/idempotency key per campaign.
fix: Persist a per-campaign progress cursor (last UserId processed) and resume from it on retry, or mark each recipient sent. Make the campaign idempotent on (CampaignId, UserId).

title: Tenant-override leakage risk on queue-trigger functions that don't clear the override before processing
where: GenerateReceiptFunction.cs:54-57, SendPushNotificationFunction.cs:56-59, SendSitewidePromoFanoutFunction.cs:71-74 (all SetTenantOverride but never ClearTenantOverride, unlike FiscalRetryService.cs:44)
impact: With host.json batchSize: 1 and a per-invocation scoped ITenantProvider, this is currently safe (each message gets a fresh scope). But the pattern is inconsistent with FiscalRetryService, and if batchSize is ever raised or the provider is registered with a wider lifetime, a tenant override from message N would leak into message N+1's reads/writes — a cross-tenant data-write bug. Relates to S8 (tenant isolation correctness).
fix: Standardize: every queue-trigger function calls ClearTenantOverride() at the top of the try (mirror FiscalRetryService). Codify as a consistency rule for Functions and add to check-consistency.mjs if checkable.

title: Error handlers log full messageText at Error level (PII-adjacent) for push/promo queues
where: SendPushNotificationFunction.cs:118-119, SendSitewidePromoFanoutFunction.cs:159-161, GenerateReceiptFunction.cs:105-106
impact: On failure the entire raw message is logged. SendPushNotificationMessage contains UserId (identifier) and Args (for promo: title/body marketing copy; for order events: orderId/orderNumber). Not strict PII, but runtime-readiness checklist item 1 says 'no PII above Debug' and IDs in error logs aggregate into App Insights retention. Low severity because no names/emails/addresses are in these payloads.
fix: Log identifiers (OrderId/UserId/EventKey) explicitly; drop the raw messageText to Debug.

title: UnitOfWorkPipelineBehavior commits even when validation fails
where: UnitOfWorkPipelineBehavior.cs:14-22 (outer) wraps ValidationPipelineBehavior.cs (inner), registered in that order at FluentValidationExtensions.cs:13-14
impact: Validation returns a failed BusinessResult (not an exception), so the outer UnitOfWork behavior still reaches await unitOfWork.CommitAsync unconditionally for any *Command. Harmless when the handler didn't run (no tracked changes), but a validator or earlier-stage code that touches the DbContext would persist on a 'rejected' command. Surfaced here because timer Functions drive these commands frequently and without a transaction boundary above them.
fix: Skip commit when the response IsFailure, or reorder so validation is the outer behavior. Architect call.

title: Rejected cleaners can still take and complete orders
where: src/Cleansia.Core.AppServices/Features/Orders/TakeOrder.cs:111-118 and CompleteOrder.cs:139
impact: The approval gate HasUploadedDocumentsAsync returns employee?.ContractStatus != ContractStatus.Pending. Employee.Reject() sets ContractStatus = Rejected (5), which is != Pending, so a rejected cleaner passes. A cleaner the admin explicitly rejected (failed vetting / documents rejected) can still claim available jobs and run them. Direct trust/liability exposure; defeats the whole approval workflow. The method name lies about what it checks.
fix: Gate on ContractStatus == Approved (and decide explicitly whether Active counts — see EMP-GAP-02). Rename the rule to reflect 'approved', give it its own BusinessErrorMessage key, and apply the same gate anywhere a cleaner acts on an order.

title: ContractStatus.Active and .Terminated are never reachable; no status lifecycle after approve/reject
where: src/Cleansia.Core.Domain/Enums/ContractStatus.cs (Active=2, Terminated=3); src/Cleansia.Core.Domain/Users/Employee.cs:229-255 (Approve, Reject); UpdateContractStatus at Employee.cs:223 (no caller); employee-detail.facade.ts:321-327 (canApproveOrReject)
impact: No command sets Active or Terminated. UpdateContractStatus() is never invoked by any handler. Admin UI canApproveOrReject() only enables actions when status is Pending, so once a cleaner is Approved or Rejected the admin can never change their status again — no off-boarding/termination, no re-instatement, no un-reject. Cannot terminate a misbehaving cleaner, cannot reverse a mistaken rejection. Half the lifecycle is dead; Active appears intended as 'approved + onboarding finished' but is fully orphaned.
fix: Decide the canonical lifecycle (likely Pending → Approved/Rejected → Active/Terminated, with allowed transitions) as an ADR; add Terminate(reason) / Reactivate() domain methods + admin commands + endpoints + UI, or delete the two unused enum values if they are not part of the model. Update the order-taking gate (EMP-GAP-01) to whatever 'can work' means.

title: Admin cannot upload, replace, or delete employee documents (3 commands, 0 endpoints)
where: EmployeeDocuments/UploadEmployeeDocument.cs, UploadNewDocumentVersion.cs, DeleteDocument.cs; Cleansia.Web.Admin/Controllers/AdminEmployeeDocumentController.cs; employee-documents.facade.ts
impact: Grep for these three commands across all *Controller*.cs returns only the partner-self SaveMyDocuments/DeleteMyDocument. The admin variants are wired nowhere. Admin docs facade has no upload/delete/new-version methods. An operator vetting a cleaner cannot attach a signed contract, upload a missing document on the cleaner's behalf, or remove a wrong/duplicate file. Back-office document management is read-only (approve/reject/download). Fully-built handlers sit unused.
fix: Add admin endpoints for upload / new-version / delete on AdminEmployeeDocumentController (with blob upload like SaveMyDocuments), then admin UI. Confirm whether UploadEmployeeDocument is meant to receive a BlobFileDto (consistency with SaveMyDocuments) — as written it expects a pre-uploaded FilePath, which no caller produces. manual_step: nswag-regen.

title: Document version-history endpoint has no UI consumer
where: AdminEmployeeDocumentController.cs:62 GetVersionHistory; EmployeeDocuments/GetDocumentVersionHistory.cs; employee-documents.facade.ts; employee-documents-section.component.html:77
impact: Auto-versioning is implemented end-to-end (EmployeeDocument.CreateNewVersion, latestVersionOnly filter, version endpoint) but the admin facade never calls .versions(...); the section template shows v{{ doc.version }} but offers no way to view prior versions. The entire versioning investment is invisible to operators; they cannot see what a cleaner replaced or audit a re-upload. Combined with EMP-GAP-03, versioning is effectively unused on the admin side.
fix: Add a 'version history' action on each document row that calls .versions(documentId) and renders prior versions (with download per version).

title: Partner web profile saves all documents as DocumentType.Other (web/mobile capability split)
where: profile/src/lib/profile/profile.facade.ts:191-198; UpdateEmployee.cs:281-289; SaveMyDocuments
impact: Web profile facade submits via employeeClient.updateEmployee(...); the monolithic UpdateEmployee.cs hardcodes DocumentType.Other for every uploaded file. The typed path SaveMyDocuments (honors DocumentType) is wired in the partner web controller but unused by the web UI. Every document a cleaner uploads on the web is typed 'Other', so admins can't tell a passport from a bank statement; the DocumentType taxonomy is meaningless for web-registered cleaners. Inconsistent with mobile, which types correctly.
fix: Web profile should upload documents via SaveMyDocuments with a per-file DocumentType picker (as mobile does), and stop piggy-backing files on UpdateEmployee. Confirm UpdateEmployee should stop accepting Documents at all.

title: Granular profile-edit endpoints exist only on mobile; web partner has monolithic-only
where: Employees/UpdatePersonalInfo.cs, UpdateIdentificationInfo.cs, UpdateAddressInfo.cs, UpdateBankDetails.cs, UpdateEmergencyContact.cs, UpdateAvailability.cs; Cleansia.Web.Mobile.Partner/Controllers/EmployeeController.cs:50-108; web partner EmployeeController.cs
impact: The six granular commands are exposed only by the mobile partner controller; web partner EmployeeController exposes none. The web profile has per-section components but the facade saves everything through one updateEmployee call requiring the whole form to be valid (onSubmit rejects if !formGroup.valid). A web cleaner cannot save one section (e.g. update only their IBAN or availability) without re-satisfying the entire onboarding form including Consent; partial edits are impossible. Per-section UI is misleading (looks editable section-by-section, isn't).
fix: Expose the six granular commands on the web partner controller and wire each profile section to its own save, matching mobile. manual_step: nswag-regen.

title: GetAvailableJobsPreview is mobile-only; web partner dashboard cannot show available-jobs preview
where: GetAvailableJobsPreview.cs + AvailableJobPreviewDto; Cleansia.Web.Mobile.Partner/Controllers/DashboardController.cs:30; web DashboardController.cs; web dashboard.facade.ts
impact: GetAvailableJobsPreview is wired only in the mobile partner DashboardController. Web DashboardController omits it; web dashboard.facade.ts shows an availableOrdersCount stat (a number) but no preview list. Feature parity gap — mobile cleaners get a 'jobs waiting for you' preview on the dashboard; web cleaners only see a count. Not broken, but a built capability the web app can't reach.
fix: Either expose on web + render a preview card, or explicitly document it as mobile-only.

title: GetAllEmployees query is dead (no endpoint) and duplicates GetPagedEmployees
where: Features/Employees/GetAllEmployees.cs
impact: Full CQRS query with validator and hand-rolled paging; grep across all controllers finds no consumer. Superseded by GetPagedEmployees (the canonical DataRangeRequest + specification form). Violates consistency A1/A3/A5 (record Query with inline Page/PageSize, hand-built Response with manual TotalPages, no specification); conventions.md: 'No dead code.' Maintenance noise, a second non-canonical way to list employees that could get wired by mistake.
fix: Delete GetAllEmployees (and its bespoke Response).

title: EmployeeListItem DTO + Employee.MapToDto() mapper are unused
where: Features/Employees/DTOs/EmployeeListItem.cs:6 and Mappers/EmployeeMappers.cs:26 MapToDto
impact: No caller (employee.MapToDto() grep in the Employees feature returns nothing; list paths use MapToAdminDto/MapToEmployeeItem). Confusing parallel 'list item' shape; risk of wiring the wrong DTO.
fix: Delete both unless a near-term consumer is planned.

title: applyGradeTemplate() is an empty stub in the admin facade
where: employee-detail/employee-detail.facade.ts:536-539
impact: Empty body with a 'will be handled by the component' comment. A public facade method that does nothing; if a button is bound to it, it silently no-ops. conventions.md: no dead code, comments explain WHY not WHAT. (Not a GAP on its own — the pay-config bulk path works via bulkApplyGrade.)
fix: Remove it, or implement the grade-template preview it gestures at.

title: AdminEmployeeDetail leaks raw ApprovedByUserId / RejectedByUserId
where: DTOs/EmployeeListItem.cs:63-65 (AdminEmployeeDetail)
impact: Raw user ids of the approving/rejecting admin are sent to the client with no display use. S4 lists 'other users' ids' as fields to scrub; the UI shows approval notes/dates, not these ids. Low (admin surface), but it's an unnecessary internal-id exposure and the pattern spreads.
fix: Either drop these fields or replace with a resolved admin display name.

title: UpdateEmployee ownership check lives in the validator (consistency B4 violation)
where: Features/Employees/UpdateEmployee.cs:39-41,183-188
impact: AllowedToUpdateEmployee (ownership) is a validator rule. consistency.md B4 explicitly names UpdateEmployee as a violation: ownership belongs in the handler (S3), not the validator. Pattern drift; ownership enforcement split from where the entity is loaded.
fix: Move the owner-match into the handler's fetch-and-guard.
